body: Inspect whether the page uses social proof signals to help uncertain visitors align their behavior with that of comparable others — including star ratings, review counts, testimonials, 'X people bought this today' counters, bestseller or 'most popular' badges, and user-generated content. Positive signals include visible aggregate review counts displayed near product names or CTAs, recognizable social proof that references similar customers (e.g., 'customers like you also bought'), and 'most popular' tier labels on pricing plans. Negative signals include absent or hidden review data, testimonials buried below the fold with no summary near the decision point, review counts that read '0 reviews' or are omitted entirely, and social counters set to zero that may signal low adoption. When social proof is absent or poorly positioned, recommend surfacing aggregated ratings and review counts near the primary CTA, adding a concise testimonial excerpt or 'most popular' badge above the fold, and referencing similarity cues (same industry, same role, same situation) to maximize relevance. Cialdini's principle holds that especially when uncertain, people will look to the actions and behaviors of others to determine their own — referencing similar others' actual behaviors proves more persuasive than self-directed appeals alone.

benchmark.standard_text: Aggregated review count and rating visible near primary CTA; at least one similarity-cued social proof signal (e.g., 'most popular', peer testimonial, or peer usage counter) present above the fold

provenance.citation_text: Social Proof: This principle indicates that 'especially when uncertain, people will look to the actions and behaviors of others to determine their own.' Observing comparable others' behavior influences individual choices. Hotels tested towel reuse messaging. Environmental benefit messaging achieved 35% compliance. However, messaging stating '75% of our guests reuse their towels' increased compliance by 26%. Most effective: '75% of people who have stayed in this room have reused their towel,' producing a 33% increase. The science demonstrates that referencing similar others' actual behaviors proves more persuasive than self-directed appeals or environmental arguments alone.

ai_review.reviewer_persona: neural-heuristic-reviewer v1.0 (top-1% senior CRO consultant; 20yr; multi-vertical D2C/SaaS/marketplace/B2B/lead-gen/fintech/media)
ai_review.why_generated: Drafter targeted Cialdini's Principle 4 (Social Proof) — peer-action signals and bestseller badges near primary CTA; one of the most-cited Cialdini applications in e-commerce.
ai_review.how_reviewed: https://www.influenceatwork.com/7-principles-of-persuasion/ (Cialdini company site — authoritative; HTTP 200 verified); "especially when uncertain, people will look to the actions and behaviors of others" verbatim from source; canonical Cialdini Principle 4.

dimension_findings.finding: Verbatim Cialdini-attributed framing of social-proof under uncertainty
6-archetype universal scope; page_type [homepage/pdp/landing/pricing] appropriate (social-proof primary on product surfaces)
no conversion-rate predictions; descriptive principle-level phrasing only (drafter self-check confirmed regex-clean)
qualitative standard_text paraphrases Cialdini guidance on aggregated peer signals + similarity cues
review-count + similarity-cued bestseller badges near CTA; quick_win effort calibration accurate
